feature: Guardrails Configuration UI over loopbackguard plugin - open-source Apache-2.0 implementation
filesToEdit: framework/configstore/migrations.go - register TableGuardrailConfig in migrations registry
transports/bifrost-http/server/server.go - wire guardrails handler route registration at line ~1433
transports/bifrost-http/server/plugins.go - add loopbackguard plugin instantiation in loadBuiltinPlugin (after line 127)
ui/app/_fallbacks/enterprise/components/guardrails/guardrailsConfigurationView.tsx - replace stub with real view import
ui/app/_fallbacks/enterprise/components/guardrails/guardrailsProviderView.tsx - replace stub with real view import
patternsToFollow: framework/configstore/tables/budget.go - GORM struct with BeforeSave validation, polymorphic ownership (multiple nullable FK pointers), config_hash tracking
framework/configstore/tables/plugin.go - JSON serialization pattern with ConfigJSON text field, BeforeSave/AfterFind encryption hooks
framework/configstore/tables/modelconfig.go - scoped configuration pattern with Scope enum, ScopeID, scope-name resolution
transports/bifrost-http/handlers/governance.go:963-1023 - RegisterRoutes pattern with /api/governance/resource CRUD endpoints and middleware chaining
transports/bifrost-http/handlers/governance.go:3141-3290 - createModelConfig handler pattern showing transaction-based creation with child entity links
ui/lib/store/apis/governanceApi.ts:55-100 - RTK-query builder pattern with providesTags/invalidatesTags cache coherence
ui/app/workspace/config/views/featureFlagsView.tsx - React view pattern with useQuery hook, mutation handlers, toast notifications, RBAC checks
plugins/loopbackguard/main.go - Plugin interface implementation (GetName, Cleanup, PreRequestHook, PreLLMHook, PostLLMHook)
steps: Step 1: Create TableGuardrailConfig GORM table in framework/configstore/tables/guardrailconfig.go with fields: ID (uuid PK), Name (string), Enabled (bool), Scope (string), ScopeID (nullable FK), GuardrailsJSON (text with polymorphic guardrail params), and polymorphic owner FK pointers (TeamID, VirtualKeyID, CustomerID nullable). Include BeforeSave validation and AfterFind JSON deserialization.

Step 2: Register TableGuardrailConfig in framework/configstore/migrations.go - add CreateTable call after TablePlugin (around line 806), add drop in rollback, add versioned migrations for any schema changes. Follow budget.go pattern at line 799-807.

Step 3: Extend configstore.ConfigStore interface (framework/configstore/store.go) with CreateGuardrailConfig, GetGuardrailConfig, UpdateGuardrailConfig, DeleteGuardrailConfig, ListGuardrailConfigs methods. Implement in rdb.go following governance handler patterns.

Step 4: Create transports/bifrost-http/handlers/guardrails.go with GuardrailsHandler struct. Implement getGuardrails, getGuardrail, createGuardrail, updateGuardrail, deleteGuardrail handlers following governance.go:3141-3290 pattern. Each handler validates scope, checks ownership, uses transaction for consistency.

Step 5: Register guardrails routes in transports/bifrost-http/handlers/guardrails.go with RegisterRoutes method (GET/POST /api/governance/guardrails, GET/PUT/DELETE /api/governance/guardrails/{id}). Call this from transports/bifrost-http/server/server.go line 1433 area.

Step 6: Add loopbackguard plugin to transports/bifrost-http/server/plugins.go loadBuiltinPlugin switch (before default at line 128): case for loopbackguard.PluginName, instantiate with MarshalPluginConfig[loopbackguard.Config], return loopbackguard.New(config).

Step 7: Wire GuardrailsHandler into transports/bifrost-http/server/server.go Bootstrap method: instantiate NewGuardrailsHandler(configStore, governanceManager), call RegisterRoutes with middleware chain (after line 1433, similar to governance handler setup).

Step 8: Implement config-to-plugin wiring: extend GovernanceManager interface with ReloadGuardrailConfig(ctx, id) that loads from DB and calls plugin's WithGuardrails or similar. Call this after guardrail config mutations.

Step 9: Create ui/lib/types/guardrails.ts with TypeScript interfaces: GuardrailConfig, GuardrailType, GuardrailParams (polymorphic union), response types for list/create/update/delete following governanceApi pattern.

Step 10: Create ui/lib/store/apis/guardrailsApi.ts with RTK-query builder: getGuardrails query, getGuardrail query, createGuardrail mutation, updateGuardrail mutation, deleteGuardrail mutation. Use providesTags=['GuardrailConfigs'] and proper invalidation on mutations.

Step 11: Create ui/app/workspace/guardrails/views/guardrailsConfigurationView.tsx with React component: display table of guardrail configs, modal forms for create/update, delete confirmation. Use useGuardrails/useMutations hooks, handle RBAC (RbacResource.GuardrailConfigs operation), show toast errors.
Step 12: Create ui/app/workspace/guardrails/views/guardrailsProviderView.tsx (if separate) showing provider-scoped guardrail assignments, or fold into guardrailsConfigurationView.

Step 13: Replace ui/app/_fallbacks/enterprise/components/guardrails/guardrailsConfigurationView.tsx: change export to import real component from '../../workspace/guardrails/views/guardrailsConfigurationView' and re-export.
Step 15: Verify route registration in transports/bifrost-http/server/server.go that guardrails handler is initialized before server starts, guardrails routes are added to router.
risks: "Risk 1: Guardrail config scope explosion - need to prevent per-VK configs from multiplying unbounded; implement pagination and soft-delete or archival. Risk 2: Plugin reload timing - when guardrail config updates via API, loopbackguard plugin must be notified synchronously; race conditions if request arrives before plugin state updates. Implement request-scoped context propagation or wait-on-reload pattern. Risk 3: Polymorphic JSON params validation - each guardrail type has different param structure; use discriminated unions in JSON schema validation. Risk 4: Backward compatibility - loopbackguard already supports in-code config (New, WithRequestGuardrails, etc.); UI configs must coexist without breaking existing patterns. Risk 5: RBAC access control - establish RbacResource.GuardrailConfigs and operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete); default enterprise-only in OSS. Risk 6: Performance on list endpoint - may have hundreds of configs per scope; add full-text search on name, pagination, and DB indexes on (scope, scope_id, enabled)."
backendEndpoints: framework/configstore/store.go:245-246 - GetPlugins/GetPlugin interface (add guardrail methods here)
framework/configstore/rdb.go - implement guardrail CRUD methods following governance pattern
loopbackguardWiring: "At runtime: (1) Server.Bootstrap loads plugins via LoadPlugins() at transports/bifrost-http/server/server.go:145. (2) loadBuiltinPlugins() calls InstantiatePlugin for each plugin name, which dispatches to loadBuiltinPlugin(). (3) For loopbackguard, switch case returns loopbackguard.New(config) or loopbackguard.NewPlugin() with guardrails attached. (4) Plugin is registered to Bifrost core config via Config.RegisterPlugin() (line 1233). (5) Request flows through PreLLMHook before provider call - loopbackguard checks each guardrail and short-circuits on violation. (6) Guardrail config updates: (a) POST/PUT to /api/governance/guardrails triggers handler, (b) handler calls configStore.UpdateGuardrailConfig and (c) governanceManager.ReloadGuardrailConfig(id) which (d) loads config from DB, (e) calls loopbackguard plugin instance to update its guardrail list, (f) in-flight requests see new config via plugin's method receiver. No restart required."
